sentences.text: Oh, much before that I have to tell you.
What happened, the Germans are [INAUDIBLE].
Well, they are.

They wanted to show the world that we, the Jewish people, are so bad [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that-- that we have others, Jewish, to be homeless, starving, and we are dressed in-- in-- look decently and ignore all those sufferers.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
What they did, they made a fake funeral.
They had six pairs of horses driven by, like, a hearse.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were to walk after that hearse.
There was no dead, just walk after the hearse and they'll make pictures.
No, that was by the [GERMAN]
To wait until more will come, and they'll make a fake funeral.
When I came out of the ghetto, and I knew that friend wants me to come to her place.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I don't know where to go.
And I looked at the horizon-- we were all the way south of Warsaw-- and I saw on the horizon red street cars, so I knew the direction I need to go.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I had very little money.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And when I came into that street car inside [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I looked very ghetto-ish-- terribly ghet-- starved, eyes scared and deep in the socket, and my face, the color was of the ground.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So I thought to myself, I have to hide that face as much as possible.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I came to one train and he said to me, whoa, you come from there.
And I was afraid that that's my end.
He'll give me away.
The next stop, I stepped down and I came to another car.
That's how people are-- some are murderers and the others are decent.
And the other one was kind.
He directed me when to get off.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A normal, kind human being.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I stepped down, and I didn't know where to go, but I was walking.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was a woman, and I asked her.
She didn't know anything.
So then I left her and I was walking.
It was a policeman, and he directed me as to where to go.
When I came there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this girl was a-- like a maid, a young-- a teenager maid.
I forgot to-- did I tell you when I was to leave the ghetto, I put on me so many pairs of stockings, one on top of the other?
Because they said when you-- when they catch you carrying extra thing, that means you are a runner away from the ghetto.

So I put on me a summer dress-- cotton with short sleeves, on top a black wool dress with long sleeves, just in case, on top a suit like you wear, a couple of sweaters, one like you wear a sweater, the other one tied-- hanging on the back and tied in the front, another one to cover me in the front and tie the sleeves in the back.
And on top of it, I put a-- they used to call it duster.
It's like a raincoat-- a light raincoat.
And that's how I left.
And I had-- I didn't want to be accused that I'm a lady.
I put-- my husband had these big handkerchiefs in color, and I put like I am a worker, go to work.
When I left?
August 28, 1942.
Yes, and I came there, and I thought the girl wouldn't know anything.
She was smart to bribe her.
I took off of me a set of very expensive underwear-- satin, very expensive.
I mean, like, decorated with satin and very expensive.
I don't see them now.
They don't have them here.
And I gave her.
And I said, Miss Polcja-- called her her name.
I said, I'm so hungry.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I had to give her some change and she went and bought me a roll and a milk.
And then I say, and I didn't wash for a long time.
I said, Miss Polcja, I need to get a shower.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she said, lady, hose behind the walls.
I thought she doesn't know, but she was-- I couldn't fool her.
I fooled myself.
And so she let me take a shower and she let me to sleep in the boy's crib.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, the boy and the mother, which is the nurse, she was that time out in the countryside somewheres.
And that boyfriend husband, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he used to work for the Germans, like, he was a truck-- he drove their truck.
And he would hide, under the engine or wherever, whatever he could buy there.
He would buy sausage.
He would buy all kinds of things and he brought.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But they-- he wouldn't come to-- and they communicated.
When he brought the stuff, he called her from that little grocery store-- Papa-Mama grocery store.
He called her and she would come and take that which he brought.
When she went to him when he called, and she told him that I am here, he wouldn't come-- for fear, he didn't come.
He wouldn't come.
Right away, he called a place which was outside Warsaw, alongside the railroad [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where they were-- I told you before, I think.
They had these summer houses.
And I answered.
He says, oh, I know now.
You are that Jew that's supposed to come.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, he-- and I'm talking now about the first war, I think.
When he was in Russia or some place there, in the trenches, used to come very-- a Russian woman much, much older than he was, and bring them something.
And so, then he took her home as the wife.
She couldn't speak a word of Polish, [LAUGHS] and he didn't speak a word of Russian.
But they communicated somewhere-- somewhat.
Yes.
And all he had in that shack, but there were the summer houses.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I would-- he let me stay there.
There is no-- no furniture of any kind, and I had all these things that I put on me.
Came in handy.
So the skirt was [LAUGHS] feet up to here, and the jacket was across from me, and that sweater was here.
That's how I stayed overnight.
He had the habit-- he used to come-- used to like to talk with people.
And she was very jealous.
She was watching there in the woods, you see, there are trees.
And she would hide behind the trees and watching as to where he's going and what he's doing.
He didn't do anything.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she went outside the gate, and the other side of the fence, there was a German Kommando.
Sonderkommando.
That means it's a section of the German power-- military or something.
They would be the killers.
Right?
And you know German, you better correct.
So she was going to call them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But if she would go and call them, they would come and destroy me and him and the child and everybody.
They-- they just would take-- maybe they would take her also.
True?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They would take all of us.
So he ran after her, brought her back to that shack.
And he grabbed her by her hair and threw her on the floor, and kicked at her, and kicked that face.
And I said, no, Mr.
Fiddler, don't do it.
He said, step away because you'll get it.
That's how mad he was.
So she was crying, and then she whimpered, and she stopped.
That's how he controlled her.
I thought, in this situation-- oh, I had a few dollars, had some money left.
So he said to me, the winter is coming and we'll-- he thought I will stay there over winter.
The winter is coming, and we'll go-- you'll get me that money and we'll go and buy provision for the winter.
Meaning currants and cabbage and potatoes and flour and what do they call those?
Cereals, everything.
I gave him the money, and when she started to pack it up like this.
Now, they had the daughter.
I told you she died.
So she would go to the cemetery.
When she went, he wanted sex from me when she's gone.
I said to myself, uh-oh.
I'm in the wrong spot.
I better get myself out of this situation.
So I took off.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As bad the face I had-- meaning, Jewish-looking face-- I just didn't think of anything.
I couldn't be there.
I went to Warsaw.
And my sister had this friend.
This friend had a step daughter, so I went to her, if I could stay there.
She says, it's not my apartment.
I couldn't keep you here.
I say, where could I go?
She said, so stay overnight.
Then, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- I went, I think, to my sister.
Did I tell you?
Did I tell you already?
No?
So I came to my sister's, and she said to me-- and she was afraid for her own life.
I told you she rented a room from a priest's sister.
Yes.
She herself was afraid, because that house was near the railroad, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and she saw how the Germans took the children-- high school children.
Those years, we wore-- we wore uniforms, navy uniforms.
How they let the children run, and they were shooting behind them, and they kept falling.
And the Poles came right away and wrapped them-- ripped off what they wore.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Then she found out-- I don't know how-- about that sanatorium for TB children.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't show you the picture?
No.
Too bad.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she went there.
At this time, I was working taking care of that Mimi, I told you.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you see, between-- between Warsaw and this town was a river.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And people had a notion, when they go behind the river, where the Soviets will stop and the Germans will come from the other side of the river.
Yes.
We went to [INAUDIBLE], to a town where Mimi was.
But did I tell you that I was in the estate?
So this midwife, she gave me to stay with her.
Remember, I told you.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she was, that time, married and she gave birth to twins, a girl and a boy.
And she said to me-- and I had in my mind who I am, in fact.
She didn't know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One day she said to me-- and this woman, the owner of the estate that I took care of her baby-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Leznicka, her name was.
And she said to midwife, she was going-- she was expecting another child, and she called the midwife to come to deliver the baby.
Yes, when I was in the-- in the woods with this lady that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that she let me stay temporary.
She was another friend of my sister's, and she let me stay for 10 days until I had my papers.
You see, what happened, it's her summer house, and her mother's summer house.
And she wouldn't let me get onto the balcony because she's afraid of her mother, that they'll inform.
So I had to stay not to show my face to her mother.
That's how people were afraid of one another.
They didn't trust one another-- their own mother.
Polish.
Just me, not anybody else.
Just me.
And I looked at her dog and I said, you see?
Your dog has the right to live, and I don't have the right to live.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, my sister had this boyfriend that I told you, and he had a friend-- a colleague, a doctor, a German, Dr.
George Tille.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this doctor had [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a sister, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and they're like friends.
And he knew of that man who would bring me the papers.
So one day at 3:00, I was supposed to be in the woods.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Regretfully, that I don't have the papers to show you.
I have it in the house.
And when you move from here to this other address, it's all in the book.
Goes to the police.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So you cannot hide.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So when they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was there, and I didn't have any of those slips, those things that shows that I came out of the ghetto, that I am registered, because I wasn't.
But there was this woman-- this woman that she-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I was with this little baby, Anthony, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she had one cow.
She had many.
This particular cow, that milks, do not mix with all the milks.
That milk is for Anthony's drink.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So she was boiling that milk in a little pot, and she put that pot-- and I had nothing to do with it.
In came the cat and drank that milk.
So she came and grabbed me like this and said, now, you better go because when my husband comes, he'll kill you.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And she grabbed a stick to hit me.
And I grabbed that stick and I say, [?
Hola, ?] that stick has two ends.
What you do to me, I'll do to you.
I was very bold.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So she ordered them to hitch the-- the horse and deliver me to town.
And I went to that midwife's place.
